subject: Careers
big_idea: Reflecting on our preferences and skills helps us identify the steps we need to take to achieve our career goals.
learning_goals: I can identify what makes a goal stronger or weaker.
I can improve a vague goal by making it clearer and more realistic.
I can create one goal with a practical next-step plan.

content.left: Goal A: Do better in school.
content.right: Goal B: Bring missing assignments up to date this week by finishing one after school each day.
prompts: Which goal would actually help someone move forward?

slides.title: A goal is not just a wish

left: A wish is something you hope for.
It may matter to you, but it does not tell you what to do next.
right: A goal is something you are working toward.
A plan is the actions that help you move toward the goal.

items: a plan for an obstacle or support

prompts: How could we improve this goal so it becomes more usable?
What could get in the way?

slides.title: What could get in the way?

content.headline: Obstacles are normal. They do not mean the goal is bad.
items: not knowing what to do first

content.model: Goal → first step → likely obstacle → support or adjustment

content.task: Write your goal, why it matters, one first step, one likely obstacle, and one support or adjustment.

items: Is my goal still too vague?
Is it too huge for right now?
Do I know my first step?
Did I plan for a likely obstacle?
Do I have a support or adjustment?

prompts: One thing that makes a goal stronger is ...
A goal is different from a wish because ...
One next action I could actually take is ...

slides.title: If we finish early: Make it stronger

content.task: Pick one weak goal and improve it, or compare two versions of the same goal and explain which one is more usable.

anchor: A strong goal helps you act.
A useful plan includes a first step, a likely obstacle, and a support or adjustment.
supported.tip: Ask: What could I actually do next?

questions.q_text: Circle the stronger goal from class and write one reason why it is stronger.
Finish the checklist: a stronger goal has a clear target, realistic scope, a reason it matters, one first step, and one support or obstacle plan.
Rewrite the weak goal Save money so it becomes more usable.
Write one goal you could actually work on. Include why it matters, one first step, one obstacle, and one support or adjustment.

self_check: Did you include what you will do first?
Did you plan for at least one obstacle?
answer_key: Goal B is stronger because it has a clear action and time frame.
Strong answers include target, time frame, first step, and support.

anchor: A stronger goal usually includes a clear target, realistic scope, a reason it matters, one first step, and a plan for a likely obstacle.
Useful goals are clear enough to guide action.

questions.q_text: Compare Goal A and Goal B from class. Which goal is more useful, and why?
List five things that make a goal stronger or more usable.
Rewrite each weak goal so it becomes more usable: Get healthier. Save money.
Create one realistic goal with a reason, one first step, one likely obstacle, and one support or adjustment. Explain why your version is stronger than a wish.

self_check: Did your revised goals become more specific and realistic?
Does your goal plan show what you can do next and how you will respond to a barrier?
answer_key: Goal B is stronger because it is clearer, more realistic, and gives an action step.
Look for clear target, realistic scope, reason it matters, first step, and obstacle/support plan.

anchor: Goal setting is not only about deciding what you want. It is about designing a plan that can survive reality: time limits, setbacks, distractions, and revision.
Use reflection on preferences and skills to build concrete steps.

questions.q_text: Explain why a strong goal is more useful than a vague wish. Use one example.
Rewrite Get healthier and Save money into stronger goals. Then explain what improved in each one.
Choose one revised goal and explain one realistic obstacle, one support, and how you would revise the plan after a setback.
Using the Big Idea wording, argue why reflecting on preferences and skills should lead to concrete steps rather than only big intentions.

self_check: Did you explain the difference between usefulness and inspiration?
Did your obstacle and support response sound realistic?
answer_key: Stronger answers show how reflection leads to action, not just intention.
A strong revision-after-setback move changes the plan instead of abandoning it.

rubrics.title: Q4 — Goal plan
rubrics.question: Create one realistic goal with a reason, one first step, one likely obstacle, and one support or adjustment. Explain why your version is stronger than a wish.

criteria.emerging: Goal is present but vague or missing major planning parts.
criteria.developing: Goal is somewhat usable but planning details are uneven or incomplete.
criteria.proficient: Goal is realistic and includes clear planning details, including a first step and obstacle response.
criteria.extending: Goal plan is precise, realistic, and shows strong practical thinking throughout.

target_understanding: Students understand that stronger goals are clear, realistic, and action-oriented.
Students understand that obstacles and revision are normal parts of planning.
Students can connect reflection on strengths and preferences to practical next steps.

watch_for.misconception: Students think a goal is just something they want.
watch_for.redirect: What could you actually do next?

watch_for.misconception: Students think changing a plan means they failed.
watch_for.redirect: How could revising the plan help you keep moving instead of stopping?

timing.activity: Warm-up comparison of weak and stronger goals
Wish vs goal vs plan and stronger-goal checklist
Revise weak goals together and independently
Personal goal-plan writing and teacher conferencing

teacher_notes: Keep the tone practical, not slogan-heavy.
Do not force private disclosure; students can choose a safe school, activity, or routine goal.
Push for usable planning language rather than vague motivation talk.
